speaker: interviewee
text: It's a lot better, your light here, than the television. They put light right into my face.

speaker: interviewer
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: It depends on the-- on the conditions, you see. I mean there's no need-- there's no need-- and also, with this equipment, the tolerances are different, and you don't really need-- you can-- you can-- you can film a lot without lights these days. Well, that's true it's--

speaker: interviewer
text: Start rolling.

speaker: interviewer
text: I think Mr. Modelski had said he'd just joined the Italian forces in Italy, didn't he? Were you listening to that? That's right, isn't it?

speaker: interviewer
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewee
text: Pardon?

speaker: interviewer
text: You just said you been-- got to Italy as we finished.

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK?

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. This is Mr. Modelski, tape three. So after you left Buchenwald, you joined the Polish forces in Italy.

speaker: interviewee
text: After I left Buchenwald, I first was working as a housekeeper with the American Army.

speaker: interviewer
text: Yes. After they dismissed us, I went to the-- what they call support forces for the Americans. That means being guards to watch for the magazines and the-- sometimes guard colonel house or whatever. But after that--

speaker: interviewee
text: After that, I'm off to Italy.

speaker: interviewer
text: How did you get to Italy?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, I didn't get permission to go through the border, so I had to go through the hills by myself. I brought some cigarettes and things, so I-- I helped to get some money for the train later on to move to Bologna, which was the place where the Polish forces were stationed.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what did you do when you got there?

speaker: interviewee
text: When I got there, they-- I asked to be joined to the Polish forces because there was still danger that the Russians might carry on with the war. It was the, they called, Cold War period. So the Polish forces started to bring people, if they could. They had a lot of German deserters and so on, who were original Polish and joined the forces. So that is the way why I joined them. But when I joined them, I also was-- knew that the war is more or less finished. I want to carry on with my studies to get some schools because I have only started schools when I was in Poland.

speaker: interviewer
text: So I joined the-- the forces and they got the mobilization papers to be moved to England. I received-- with many others, we were moved by train, and later on boat to England. We were moved to English places which I'm-- can't remember before we were put into what they call a resettlement corps, which was supposed to train us for work in the factories or whatever. Some of us were learning English. My intention was to get to Polish grammar school to finish my education. And in order to do that, I had to move to a place where there was a Polish grammar school, which was moved from Italy to England, to continue the work of teaching, which was between Brandon and Tetford, on a place called Bodney Airfield. In Norfolk?

speaker: interviewee
text: In Norfolk. There I finished my studies, received matriculation, and then I was-- I went to Yorkshire to get a school, which could accommodate me to carry on with finishing my studies, because matriculation doesn't give me any profession yet. So I joined-- went to the Halifax Municipal Technical College. They call it later on College of Technology and Science, which was teaching engineering, textiles, and so on. I picked the subject which I took-- I thought I can manage to learn. My English wasn't very perfect then but I've got the permission from the school chairman to be accepted, which I sent to London special committee which gave grants for studying. And I have received a grant for three years study, which I've completed successfully, receiving a charted technologies of textile industry. And from then on, I first had to look for certain jobs. Sometimes I had to start from scrap doing a weaving job or spinning job till later on I got myself familiar with the procedure. I got a better job. My last job was as a quality control in the textile industry, which was a very important job because on the quality depended the sale of the product. And that I continued till I retired.

speaker: interviewer
text: How did you first adjust to life in England?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, it didn't have any effect on me, although I couldn't quite get on with the people at the beginning. My-- whenever I spoke, they knew right away that I am foreigner. I know in one bed and breakfast place I went, a chap seeing written on my uniform Poland, he started saying-- trying to be nice to me. Poland was the first to fight the Germany! Which was right, but I said to him, yes, and at the beginning, we didn't get any help at all, till we were completely capitulated. And that's how it went.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you find the way of life very strange?

speaker: interviewee
text: At the beginning, I found it strange because of different monetary system. Even at work, they treated foreigners-- in this country, at the beginning, were treated as something alien. And it was difficult for a foreigner to get a job because you heard right away shouting, this foreigner got a job and I'm here doing this and that? So I knew there was a difference. Now it is different because they have done-- they have done many foreign people here that-- and there is certain law now which forbids the treatment of foreign students or foreigners.

speaker: interviewer
text: What about your domestic circumstances?

speaker: interviewee
text: My domestic circumstance? First of all, I got married with a Polish lady which, after two years living with me, received a cancer-- breast cancer and died. So that was rather sudden to me. I had a son with her which also had a physical disability, what they call muscular dystrophy, and had to be moved to special place where you were looked after, and he died after 18 years of his life. So I've married later on an English lady, which was a lot younger than I was. At the beginning, life was going very good. I had three children with her, but she later on-- this country is known for being the biggest country for divorces, and she divorced me. Well, she didn't divorce me, but she went away and lived somewhere else. So I divorced her, in a way, because I wanted to carry on with married life. So I got married later on with Polish lady, Magda, and we live, so far, happily.

speaker: interviewer
text: So how many children have you had by your three wives?

speaker: interviewee
text: By my first wife, I had one child which died. Then I had three children and with my present wife, I have one child. That is, all together, five.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did it ever occur to you to, instead of coming to England, to go to live in Poland at the end of the war?

speaker: interviewee
text: It did occur to me at the beginning when I didn't have any studies, but hearing of the difficulties of the-- of the regime and so on, I-- I knew that living under Communist regime wouldn't be very happy. And once I managed to study in this country, I adapted myself to the living condition. And I thought, I can survive here without looking for a job in Poland or whatever. Because under those conditions they lived in there, I knew I wouldn't be very happy.

speaker: interviewer
text: Have you been back to Poland at all to see your mother and sisters?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, I went in 1960 or '65, and I knew they were very happy. It was nice living in there, but I couldn't adjust myself to the conditions they live on, where money was worthless and living was just from day to day. In here, where you live, you know the money got some value. And when you work, you can get somewhere. In there, I didn't see the future very bright.

speaker: interviewer
text: Have you been back to Auschwitz?

speaker: interviewee
text: I went Auschwitz, yes. When I was to-- you know, went to Poland, I went to Auschwitz. And I had examined the main camp, and I also went to the place where I worked-- the-- the farm. In the main camp, I had examined the places of torture, which I didn't see, and I've seen the [NON-ENGLISH], what they call-- the penalty where they put in a square, one square meter, four people overnight. And I also seen the-- where they-- they used to keep the people for death penalty regarding escapees, which they put them down through hunger. I examined these things, and I've-- the-- and I also seen the various bags of hair being put and put into bags to show where they were removed, and also the limbs and spare parts of legs and things.

speaker: interviewer
text: Why did you go back to Auschwitz?

speaker: interviewee
text: I wanted to see how it looks now to me. And I also wanted to see the place where I worked in the farm, the chicken farm. They used to-- they-- they had for chickens nowadays, I found people living in those. And I asked them, have you got no houses? No, these are the best accommodation we can have so far. So I went to have a look how it looks after.

speaker: interviewer
text: What effect did going back have on you?

speaker: interviewee
text: A bit of sad memory. That's all. But it didn't affect me very much because I was young, and I-- I didn't have any feeling about the things that happened in the past.

speaker: interviewer
text: Looking back on those times now, how do you think you managed to survive?

speaker: interviewee
text: The main thing which I managed, due-- due to my stay in Auschwitz, was that I was young, I was healthy, and I had a bit of help. My brothers-- older brother-- who was veterinary surgeon, he managed to get a job in the camp. And he helped me at the beginning to get through the old, hard period. Once I'd gone through that, accustomed myself, physically, to the procedure of camp hardship.

speaker: interviewer
text: How do you think those experiences have shaped your thinking today?

speaker: interviewee
text: When I do remember, I write a lot of articles-- some of them in English, some of them in Polish-- because a lot of people don't realize what happened there. And there's still a lot of bad people who may have an effect on others. There's a lot of hatred between people. I remember when I came to this country, why they didn't like foreigners? I couldn't understand this. In America, there's all foreigners, and they seem to be living happily. Why in this country?

speaker: interviewer
text: I remember now the Pakistani are come. Ooh, colored people, they don't like them. Same as Negroes, Jamaican. In my way, these things have changed now in this country because there is certain law now which protects the people. And if they go for work, if they're refused the work, they have to explain why. Is it because of racial [INAUDIBLE]? So that has-- a camp has no effect on me altogether. What do you think the world has learned from the Holocaust?

speaker: interviewee
text: That I don't know much about it. I do write in order to give people the-- the reminder of what I've passed. And some of these horrible things are even difficult to describe, and I do not-- cannot explain them why another human being can be so nasty, sadistic, to another one, to see that he can gain something on somebody else's misfortune.

speaker: interviewer
text: Well, thank you very much. Is there anything that you'd like to-- to add to what you've said at all?

speaker: interviewee
text: There's nothing else I can add to it except I do keep remaining my articles in Polish and English that the horrors of Auschwitz should never be repeated. And there should be people treated like human beings, in harmony and love. That's the one thing.

speaker: interviewer
text: Thank you very much.

speaker: interviewee
text: That's it?

speaker: interviewer
text: Thank you.

speaker: interviewee
text: Didn't take that long.

speaker: interviewer
text: Well-- bit about the-- [INAUDIBLE].

speaker: interviewee
text: This is my wife, Magda, whom I have married 25 years ago. She has brought a child with her, and we have a child of our own. We lived very happy and are hoping to live longer and longer happily. During-- after the war, I've tried to get some compensation from the German government for the times I've stayed in concentration camps. I tried to get legal aid from German government. They gave me the aid, but they court verdict was that if I was a Jew, persecuted for racially or religious opinions, or if I were forced labor, then I might have had a chance. Being a political prisoner, I had no chance at all. Although, I have stayed in the American army and with the Americans after the war, they paid no contribution to pension. Therefore, the German government said I have no entitlement whatsoever. We are here, settled very well, and we think that life in England is very good.

speaker: interviewer
text: Yes, yes. I have-- mine's black. All right.

speaker: interviewer
text: All right, [? Fil, ?] do you want to talk [? about? ?]

speaker: interviewee
text: This is the picture of my family in 1938. The first picture from the left, it's myself in scout uniform. Then at the back row, there's a relative of ours. Then is my twin brother, then my brother in cadet uniform. Then going to the next row is my sister, Barbara, my mother, Stanislawa. My father, Zygmunt, and my older brother, Izydor. And in the first row is my youngest brother, Stanislaw.

speaker: interviewer
text: One shot of him. OK. Yeah. OK.

speaker: interviewee
text: This is my father who was photographed in year 1941 in Auschwitz. In 1942, April, he died in Birkenau. [PAUSES FOR 9 SECONDS] This is my Polish identification card made during the occupation in Germany and a picture of me before I was arrested.

speaker: interviewer
text: That's fine.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK, that's fine.

speaker: interviewee
text: This is my personal card, taken from the German files in Buchenwald. It's a picture of me, which was made in Germany in Auschwitz, which was transferred later on to Buchenwald. Sit down. Get down! This is the number, 3476 tattooed on my arm at arrival at Auschwitz. [PAUSES FOR 6 SECONDS] This is the black wall where all the executions were performed. I used to live on block number 11, on a blocked number 10, rather. And through the windows, which had no wooden shutters at that time, through the air vents, I could see an execution of a family-- two-- a-- a woman and a man and two children being shot through the back of the head. [PAUSES FOR 14 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewer
text: Here we go.

speaker: interviewee
text: This is my younger twin brother. Picture made in Poland after the war. [PAUSES FOR 8 SECONDS] This is the picture of myself after receiving a diploma in textile technology at the chartered textile technology in year 1972.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK.

speaker: interviewee
text: This is the picture of my wife, Magda, my stepson, standing with blue jumper, and bending down, my son. This picture was taken in 1991 in Huddersfield.

speaker: interviewer
text: That's fine, thank you.
